1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
212	
213	## Directive Format
214	
215	```markdown
216	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
217	
218	## Slug
219	<slug>
220	
221	## Strategy snapshot
222	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
223	
224	## Routes
225	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
226	
227	## References
228	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
229	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
230	
231	## Focus areas (optional)
232	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
233	
234	## Known issues
235	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
236	```
237	
238	## What you do
239	
240	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
241	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
242	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
243	   - Read the entire chapter.
244	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
245	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
246	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
247	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
248	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
249	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
250	7. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
251	8. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
252	
253	You may also use:
254	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
255	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
256	
257	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
258	
259	## Report format
260	
261	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
262	
263	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
264	
265	Concretely:
266	
267	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
268	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
269	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
270	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
271	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
272	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
273	
274	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
275	
276	```markdown

278

280	<slug>
281

283	<NNN>

285	## Top-level summaries
286

289	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
290	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
291	
292	### Proofs lacking detail
293	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
294	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
295	
296	### Lean difficulty quality
297	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
298	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
299	
300	### Multi-route coverage
301	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
302	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
303	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
304	
305	### Citation discipline
306	<bullets naming chapter + label + which citation element is missing or suspect. Omit when zero findings.>
307	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: `% SOURCE:` line has no `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical — writer did not name the local file. Likely fabrication.
308	- `Bar.tex` / `\def:scheme`: `% SOURCE:` claims `(read from references/hartshorne-II-1.md)` but that file does not exist on disk. Fabrication; writer must dispatch a retriever or remove the citation.
309	
310	## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals
311	
312	<One proposal block per strategy phase with no or stub blueprint coverage. Each block is a complete chapter outline as specified in "Unstarted-phase proposals" above — not a one-line flag. Omit this section only when every phase has adequate coverage.>
313	
314	### Proposed chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`
315	
316	**Covers**: `<Lean file path(s)>`
317	**Strategy phase**: <phase name>
318	**Why now**: <one sentence>
319	
320	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
321	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <...>
322	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
323	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
324	
325	**`\uses` skeleton**:
326	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
327	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
328	
329	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
330	
331	**References for writer**:
332	- `references/<slug>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
333	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name>">
334	
335	**Subphase choices exposed**:
336	- <choice description and trade-off, or omit field if single decomposition>
337	
338	## Per-chapter
339	
340	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
341	- **complete**: true | partial | false
342	- **correct**: true | partial | false
343	- **notes**:
344	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
345	  - ...
346	
347	### blueprint/src/chapters/Bar.tex — complete + correct, no notes.
348	
349	(Cover every chapter. Chapters with findings get the full multi-line
350	block. Chapters that are `complete: true`, `correct: true`, with
351	no notes worth surfacing get the compact one-liner.)
352	
353	## Cross-chapter notes
354	
355	- `<chapter A>` defines `\foo` but `<chapter B>`'s proof of `\bar` uses a stronger version.
356	- ...
357	
358	(Omit if empty.)
359	
360	## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)
361	
362	If you found a definition that, on close reading, conflicts with the strategy in a way that requires a strategy change (not just a chapter rewrite), name it here.
363	
364	- `<chapter A>` / `\def:foo`: as currently defined, this <conflict>. STRATEGY.md says <X>, but `foo` actually does <Y>.
365	
366	## Severity summary
367	
368	Apply these rules verbatim:
369	
370	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
371	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
372	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
373	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter.
374	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
375	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels.
376	  - **Citation-discipline findings on blocks feeding an active prover route**.
377	  - **Unstarted-phase proposals** — each proposed chapter lands here as `unstarted-phase proposal: <phase name> — dispatch blueprint-writer for <proposed chapter filename> or record deferral`. These are must-act because an unwritten blueprint is an unparallelisable, unpreviewable phase; writing it early is the cheapest action in the loop.
378	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:
379	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.
380	  - Citation-discipline findings on blocks NOT feeding an active prover route.
381	  - Informational cross-chapter style issues.
382	- **informational** — minor observations: naming drift, optional `\lean{...}` references to helpers worth promoting, low-impact prose suggestions.
383	
384	Overall verdict: one sentence. If unstarted-phase proposals exist, the verdict must name them — "N phases have no blueprint coverage; proposals provided for immediate writer dispatch" is a required component of the verdict when N > 0.
385	```
386	
387	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter. Unstarted-phase proposals do not block existing provers — they trigger blueprint-writer dispatch for future prover work. Do not under-classify them as "informational"; the project pays in deferred parallelism every iter a phase sits without a blueprint.
388	
389	## Return value
390	
391	Your final assistant message:
392	
393	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> chapters audited, <M> findings, <K> unstarted-phase proposals`
394	- Top-level summary counts (incomplete parts, proofs lacking detail, unstarted phases, etc.)
395	- The path to your full report.
396	
397	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
398	
399	## Reminders
400	
401	- **Read every chapter, no scope shortcuts.**
402	- **Cross-reference every strategy phase against existing chapters.** An unstarted phase is not a clean bill of health — it is an opportunity to write the blueprint now and enable parallelism.
403	- **Proposals are actionable, not informational.** Each proposal block must be concrete enough to serve as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Vague "chapter needed for X" notes are not proposals.
404	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
405	- **You audit against the directive's context**, not your own ideas of what the project should look like — but you ARE critical of weak prose and under-specified Lean hints.
406	- **You flag, you don't fix.** Even when the fix is obvious, the plan agent decides what changes next iter.
407	- **The per-chapter shape is fixed**: `complete`, `correct`, `notes`. Don't reshape. The plan agent depends on this format.

1	# blueprint-writer — pic0-identity-component-chapter
2	
3	## Iter
4	
5	184
6	
7	## Why this dispatch
8	
9	iter-183 blueprint-reviewer flagged this as a **must-fix-this-iter
10	unstarted-phase proposal**: A.3 `Pic⁰` identity + degree has zero
11	blueprint coverage, blocking the A.2.c → A.3 → A.4.d pipeline. Writing
12	the chapter unblocks the substrate construction
13	(`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` is "NEW PROJECT MATERIAL" per
14	`STRATEGY.md`) for downstream Lean work, and lets the Albanese-UP
15	wiring in A.4.d.ii consume `Pic⁰` once it lands.
16	
17	## Chapter to create
18	
19	**File**: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`
20	
21	**Strategy phase**: A.3 (`Pic⁰` identity + degree). Iters left ~16–28
22	per `STRATEGY.md`. Substrate `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` is
23	UNOWNED (NEW PROJECT MATERIAL).
24	
25	**Single .lean covered**: `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean`
26	(file does NOT yet exist; skeleton owed iter-185+; this chapter is the
27	authoritative spec).
28	
29	## Content scope (the writer's task)
30	
31	Write the chapter at textbook-rigour level using the references named
32	below. The chapter must contain at least these declarations (in
33	dependency order), each with the citation discipline below:
34	
35	1. **`\definition{IdentityComponentGroupScheme}`** —
36	   `\label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}`,
37	   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}`. For a
38	   group scheme `G` over a base `S` with identity section `e : S → G`
39	   factoring through `G`, the **identity component** `G⁰` is the
40	   open-and-closed subgroup scheme of `G` whose underlying topological
41	   space is the connected component of the image of `e`.
42	2. **`\theorem{IdentityComponentOpenSubgroup}`** —
43	   `\label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}`,
44	   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}`.
45	   For a group scheme `G` locally of finite type over a field `k`, the
46	   identity component `G⁰` is an open subgroup scheme.
47	3. **`\definition{Pic0Subscheme}`** —
48	   `\label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}`,
49	   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}`.
50	   `Pic⁰_{C/k} := (Pic_{C/k})⁰`, the identity component of the Picard
51	   scheme.
52	4. **`\definition{DivisorDegreePic}`** —
53	   `\label{def:divisor_degree_pic}`,
54	   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}`. The degree map
55	   `deg : Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ` extracted from the Hilbert-polynomial
56	   decomposition of `Pic_{C/k}`.
57	5. **`\theorem{Pic0IsAbelianVariety}`** —
58	   `\label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}`,
59	   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}`.
60	   `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group
61	   scheme of dimension `g(C)` — an abelian variety of dimension `g`.
62	
63	`\uses` skeleton (cross-chapter):
64	- `thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` uses
65	  `def:identity_component_group_scheme`.
66	- `def:pic_zero_subscheme` uses `def:identity_component_group_scheme`,
67	  `def:pic_scheme` (from `Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex`).
68	- `def:divisor_degree_pic` uses `def:pic_scheme` (from
69	  `Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex`) and any `def:hilbert_polynomial`
70	  pin (from `Picard_QuotScheme.tex`).
71	- `thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety` uses `def:pic_zero_subscheme`,
72	  `thm:identity_component_open_subgroup`, `def:divisor_degree_pic`,
73	  `def:genus` (from `Genus.tex`), `thm:fga_pic_representability`
74	  (from `Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex`).
75	
76	## Design choice (Choice A vs Choice B, per iter-183 review)
77	
78	**Use Choice A**: build `Pic⁰` as the open-and-closed identity-component
79	subgroup scheme of the abstract group scheme `Pic_{C/k}`, via the
80	abstract `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` API. This keeps the substrate
81	gap localised to the identity-component construction (Stacks tag
82	0B7R / 0B7T) and avoids coupling `Pic⁰`'s definition to the degree
83	map.
84	
85	**Substrate keeps in chapter**: the underlying
86	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` construction stays in this chapter
87	(rather than being split off into a separate
88	`GroupScheme_IdentityComponent.tex`), with a `% NOTE: future split
89	candidate when a second consumer materialises` marker. iter-183
90	reviewer rationale: substrate is small (~200–300 LOC), a separate
91	chapter is premature.
92	
93	## Citation discipline (HARD RULE)
94	
95	You MUST follow the citation discipline from `.archon/prompts/plan.md`
96	exactly:
97	
98	1. Every theorem / definition block has `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read
99	   from references/<file>)` BEFORE the `\begin{...}` environment.
100	2. Every block has `% SOURCE QUOTE: <verbatim original-language text>`
101	   BEFORE the environment.
102	3. Every block has `\textit{Source: ...}` as the first prose line
103	   INSIDE the environment.
104	4. Every proof block has `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: <verbatim>` BEFORE
105	   `\begin{proof}` (when source provides a proof — for substrate
106	   constructions where the source is an existence theorem rather than
107	   a proof transcript, use `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate
108	   construction — source proves existence; assembled here from
109	   <list>)`).
110	5. NO source from memory — you must actually open the local source
111	   file under `references/` and copy verbatim. If the file you need
112	   is not on disk, dispatch the `reference-retriever` subagent
113	   (your `--write-domain` allows this) to fetch it FIRST, then come
114	   back and quote.
115	
116	## Sources you should consult
117	
118	Open these local files (your `--write-domain` lets you read them all):
119	
120	1. `references/abelian-varieties.pdf` (Milne *Abelian Varieties*
121	   2008 course notes) — §III.6 (the Albanese / Picard scheme of a
122	   curve), specifically Prop 6.1 / Thm 6.4. This is your primary
123	   source for declarations 3 and 5.
124	2. `references/kleiman-picard.pdf` (Kleiman *The Picard scheme*
125	   FGA Explained / arXiv:math/0504020) — §5 (Hilbert-polynomial
126	   decomposition of `Pic_{X/S}`) and §6 (the identity component
127	   construction). Primary source for declarations 1 and 4.
128	3. `references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf` (Mumford *Abelian
129	   Varieties*) — §III.6 + §III.13 if Milne is too thin on the
130	   smoothness-in-char-p case.
131	4. **You will likely also need**: Stacks tag 0B7R / 0B7T for the
132	   abstract group-scheme identity-component construction. Check
133	   whether `references/stacks-*.md` cache already covers these tags;
134	   if not, dispatch `reference-retriever` to fetch the Stacks tex
135	   (your `--write-domain` allows this).
136	5. Optional: any `references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.md` (if
137	   present) → IV.4 for the Jacobian as an abelian variety in the
138	   char-0 case.
139	
140	## Proof strategy guidance
141	
142	The identity-component construction extracts the open-and-closed
143	subscheme containing `η_J` from representability of `Pic_{C/k}` as a
144	disjoint union of quasi-projectives (Theorem 4.1 of
145	`Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex` already pins this disjoint-union
146	structure). Dimension is `g` by Riemann–Roch / Serre duality on
147	`Pic⁰` (Milne III.6); smoothness in characteristic `p` requires
148	care (Milne IV §13–14) but in char 0 is automatic. Geometric
149	irreducibility follows because `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is the connected
150	component of the identity by construction. The degree map is the
151	index of the Hilbert-polynomial component containing a given class
152	(Kleiman §5).
153	
154	## Markers — DO NOT write

157	  `sync_leanok` phase adds them based on the actual Lean source
158	  status. You write the chapter; the loop manages `\leanok`.
159	- **Do NOT add `\mathlibok` markers** anywhere. The review agent
160	  manages those.

165	- Do NOT write Lean code or speculate about specific Mathlib
166	  lemma names beyond `[expected]` hints already given.
167	- Do NOT introduce new strategy decisions; if you spot a
168	  strategy-modifying finding, surface it under

171

173	
174	You do NOT need to add `\input{chapters/Picard_IdentityComponent}` to
175	`content.tex` — the planner will do that manually after the chapter
176	lands, as a single-line update.
177	
178	## Verification
179	
180	After writing, your report should record:
181	- Citation count per source file (verifies you actually opened each
182	  file claimed in `% SOURCE: ... (read from ...)`).
183	- Whether you needed `reference-retriever` (and if so, what slugs).
184	- Any strategy-modifying findings.
185	- A summary of the 5 declarations + 5 proofs landed.

3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the `k`-rational pointing of a smooth proper
9	geometrically irreducible curve `C / k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis.
10	End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms. **Char-general:** the
11	protected signatures take `[Field k]` only — NO `CharZero`. Any char-0
12	sub-case route is therefore PARTIAL only.

14	The witness OBJECT `J` is always real (constructed unconditionally); only
15	`isAlbaneseFor` is universally quantified over `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`. Spine:
16	**pointed vs. unpointed**. `genus C := \dim_k H^1(C, O_C)` (arithmetic
17	genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined
11	% that Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) ships every
12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight $=$ Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      $=$ localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point $z$ of a scheme $X$ has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at $z$}, written
37	$\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$, is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$. The
39	\emph{coheight} of $z$ in $X$, written $\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)$, is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of $X$: the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in $X$, where $\prec$ is the
43	strict specialisation order $x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x$.
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at $z$ counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of $X$ containing $z$ --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-$1$ use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-$1$ hypothesis on $Y$ alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a $\Spec$ with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-$1$ specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.
88	
89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	
101	\leanok
102	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
103	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
104	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
105	  Let $X$ be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
106	  let $U \subseteq X$ be an open subset. For any $z \in U$,
107	  \[
108	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
109	  \]
110	  where on the right $U$ carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
111	  z \in U\rangle$ denotes $z$ viewed as a point of $U$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	
116	\leanok
117	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers $n$, of the
118	  set of $n$ for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
119	  $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in the relevant
120	  specialisation preorder (with $\prec$ denoting strict specialisation). It
121	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
122	  strict-generisation chains starting at $z$ in $X$ and those starting at the
123	  corresponding point of $U$.
124	
125	  \emph{Chains in $U$ embed into $X$.} The inclusion $U \hookrightarrow X$ is
126	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if $y \rightsquigarrow x$ in $U$
127	  then $y \rightsquigarrow x$ in $X$, and distinct points of $U$ remain
128	  distinct in $X$. Hence every strict-generisation chain in $U$ starting at
129	  $z$ lifts to one in $X$ of the same length.
130	
131	  \emph{Chains in $X$ starting at $z$ all lie in $U$.} Conversely, suppose
132	  $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ is a strict-generisation chain
133	  in $X$ with $a_0 = z \in U$. For each $i \geq 0$, the relation $a_i \succeq
134	  z$ (in the specialisation preorder) is $z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
135	  $a_i$ is in the closure of $\{z\}$ \emph{viewed from above}; in our
136	  convention the strict order $a \prec b$ records $b \rightsquigarrow a$
137	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so $a_i \succeq z$ in $X$ means
138	  $a_i \rightsquigarrow z$ in $X$. By the Mathlib lemma
139	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
140	  $a \rightsquigarrow z$ with $z$ in an open set $U$ forces $a \in U$, since
141	  $U$ is open and contains $z$, hence contains every point whose closure
142	  contains $z$ --- equivalently every $a$ with $a \rightsquigarrow z$),
143	  every $a_i$ lies in $U$. Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
144	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
145	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle$ in $U$ of the same length.
146	
147	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
148	  chains starting at $z$ (resp.\ at $\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$) and
149	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
153	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
154	
155	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
156	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
157	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
158	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
159	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	
163	\leanok
164	  [Coheight on $\Spec R$ equals height in $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R$]
165	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
166	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
167	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring and let $p \in \Spec R$ (viewed as a point of
168	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
169	  \[
170	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
171	  \]
172	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
173	  prime-ideal chains $\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}$
174	  ending at the prime $\mathfrak{p}$ corresponding to $p$.
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	\leanok
180	  The specialisation preorder on $\Spec R$ is the dual of the natural
181	  inclusion preorder on $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$. Concretely, for
182	  $p, q \in \Spec R$ with underlying primes $\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}$,
183	  the relation $p \leq q$ in the specialisation preorder (which is
184	  $q \rightsquigarrow p$ in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
185	  containment $\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}$. This is the content of
186	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
187	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
188	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on $\Spec R$
189	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R$).
190	
191	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of $p$ in $\Spec R$
192	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at $p$,
193	  i.e.\ ascending chains $p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in the
194	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of $\mathfrak{p}$ in
195	  $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$ (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
196	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
197	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
198	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
199	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
200	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
201	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
202	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
203	  identification $\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}$
204	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
205	\end{proof}
206	
207	\section{The coheight $=$ Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
208	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
209	
210	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
211	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
212	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
213	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
214	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
215	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
216	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
217	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
218	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
219	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
220	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}
223	
224	\leanok
225	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
226	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
228	  \uses{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding, lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
229	  Let $X$ be a scheme and let $z \in X$ be any point. Then
230	  \[
231	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
232	  \]
233	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at $z$ equals the coheight of $z$ in
234	  the underlying topological space of $X$ (with respect to the specialisation
235	  preorder).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  \uses{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding, lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
240	  \leanok
241	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
242	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
243	
244	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of $z$.} By
245	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
246	  applied to the open set $X$ itself, there exists an affine open
247	  $U \subseteq X$ with $z \in U$. Write $\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U$
248	  for $z$ viewed as a point of the subscheme $U$.
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to $z$ inside $U$.} Since
251	  $U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ via the affine equivalence
252	  $U \simeq \Spec R$ (with $R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$), the point
253	  $\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$ corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
254	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
255	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,$\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$.
256	
257	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of $\Gamma(U)$ at
258	  $\mathfrak{p}$.} The classical affine-stalk identification says
259	  $\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}$. In Mathlib this is the
260	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
261	  $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ as a localisation of $R$ at $\mathfrak{p}$ in the
262	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
263	
264	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
265	  $\mathfrak{p}$.} The Mathlib lemma
266	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
267	  \[
268	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
269	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
270	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
271	  \]
272	  where $\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})$ is the prime-ideal height in
273	  $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$. By
274	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
275	  $\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}$ used in
276	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
277	
278	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from $U$ to $X$.} By
279	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to $R$ and the
280	  affine identification $U \simeq \Spec R$,
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
283	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
284	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
285	  \]
286	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
287	  $U \subseteq X$ promotes the right-hand side back to
288	  $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)$. Concatenating the equalities from
289	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{$\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1$}{KrullDimLE 1}}
293	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
294	
295	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
296	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
297	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
298	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-$1$ case used in
299	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
300	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
301	
302	\begin{lemma}
303	
304	\leanok
305	  [Coheight $= 1$ forces stalk $\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1$]
306	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
308	  \uses{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
309	  Let $X$ be a scheme and let $z \in X$ be a point with
310	  $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$. Then the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$
311	  has Krull dimension at most $1$, equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
312	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
313	\end{lemma}
314	
315	\begin{proof}
316	  \uses{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
317	  \leanok
318	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
319	  $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$.
320	  The Mathlib predicate $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R$ unfolds to
321	  $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1$, so the inequality $1 \leq 1$
322	  discharges the goal.
323	\end{proof}
324	
325	\section{Consumer wiring and the Stacks 00TT carve-out}
326	\label{sec:coheight_consumers}
327	
328	\paragraph{Consumer 1: the \texttt{hreg\_dim} refactor in
329	\texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}}{the codim-1 extension chapter}.}
330	The private helper
331	\texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot}
332	inside that chapter's Lean target currently carries an internal
333	\texttt{sorry} of the form
334	\[
335	  \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\;\wedge\;
336	  \operatorname{ringKrullDim}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z} = 1.
337	\]
338	After this chapter's
339	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} (and its underlying
340	\cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}) land, the conjunction splits into
341	two independent goals:
342	\begin{itemize}
343	  \item The right conjunct $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1$
344	    is discharged by
345	    $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$ (the standing codim-$1$ hypothesis
346	    on $z$) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
347	    pair $\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1$ (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
348	    and $\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1$ (also from the bridge, since
349	    $1 \leq 1$) gives equality.
350	  \item The left conjunct $\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$
351	    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
352	    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
353	    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
354	    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A $\to$ IsRegularLocalRing $A_{\mathfrak{p}}$} at
355	    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is $\sim 200$--$300$~LOC of
356	    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
357	    chapter).
358	\end{itemize}
359	Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
360	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
361	one remains as a named Mathlib gap.
362	
363	\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
364	hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
365	The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
366	argument
367	\[
368	  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
369	\]
370	at every use site. Because $Y$ is a prime divisor of $X$ (so
371	$\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1$ by the definition of a prime
372	divisor as a codim-$1$ irreducible closed subset),
373	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
374	automatically from the codim-$1$ hypothesis on $Y$, so the explicit instance
375	argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.
376	
377	\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-$1$ infrastructure.} Any subsequent
378	project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-$1$ point has
379	Krull dim $\leq 1$'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-$1$
380	argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
381	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
382	The instance form
383	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
384	typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.
385

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
20	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	\Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$ and the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~$0$
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct $\chi$ — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct $\chi$
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let $D$ be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% $H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*$;
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-$0$ critical path requires. The
45	% direct $\chi$ proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% $\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g$, and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity $\chi(k(P)) = 1$.
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-$0$ carve-out vs. general $g$ — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} to the genus-$0$ specialisation
54	% $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$. The general-$g$
55	% statement $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor $K$ and Serre
57	% duality / the $K - D$ pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-$0$ critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate $\chi$-identity
61	% \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} is stated and
62	% sketched for general $g$ (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-$g$ curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general $g$ and may close the body initially
67	% under a $g = 0$ hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
70	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely $g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$
82	via the project's $\Module\bar k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  \uses{def:genus}
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $\chi(\mathcal F)$
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
99	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $\mathcal F$
106	  be a coherent sheaf on $C$. The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  $\mathcal F$ is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  $\dim C = 1$, Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  $H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0$ for $i \geq 2$, so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of $\mathcal F$ on the proper $\bar
120	  k$-scheme $C$ guarantees that both $H^0$ and $H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional $\bar k$-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  \Cref{def:genus}), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf $\mathcal F$ on $C$ and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's $\Module \bar k$-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper $\HModule \bar k$ (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) applied to a
131	  $\Module \bar k$-flavoured carrier built from $\mathcal F$, with
132	  $\dim_{\bar k}$ extracted via $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$. The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$ and from the $\Ext$-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The $\ell$-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[$\ell$-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:eulerChar_curve}
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $l(D)$ and of the
149	  % "dimension of $|D|$") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote $\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))$ by $l(D)$,
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of $|D|$} is $l(D) - 1$. The number
153	  % $l(D)$ is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $l(D)$).}
155	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let
156	  $D \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}). The
157	  \emph{$\ell$-invariant of $D$} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the $\bar k$-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to $D$. Finiteness of
164	  $\ell(D)$ is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper $\bar k$-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system $|D|$} has
167	  $\bar k$-dimension $\ell(D) - 1$ as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; $\ell(D)$ is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  $D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}$ (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}) and returns
174	  $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))$,
175	  computed through the same $\Module \bar k$-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve}. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference $\mathcal O_C(D)$ by name and consume only its
180	  $H^0$ functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}
