183	\section{The $\chi$ identity: $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$}
184	
185	\begin{theorem}
186	
187	
188	\leanok
189	[Euler-characteristic identity for $\mathcal O_C(D)$]
190	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
192	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
193	        thm:divisor_degree_hom}
194	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
195	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any $D$, $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
196	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
199	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
200	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
202	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
203	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
204	  % is the Euler characteristic
205	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
206	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
207	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
208	  $\bar k$ of genus $g = g(C)$, and let $D \in \Div(C)$. Then
209	  \[
210	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
211	  \]
212	\end{theorem}
213	
214	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case $D = 0$. Then our
215	% formula says
216	% $\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g$,
217	% because $H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k$ for any projective variety
218	% (I, 3.4), and $\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g$ by (1.1).
219	% Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point. We will show
220	% that the formula is true for $D$ if and only if it is true for
221	% $D + P$. Since any divisor can be reached from $0$ in a finite number
222	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
223	% the result holds for all $D$.
224	% We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its structure sheaf is
225	% the skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$, which we denote by
226	% $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$ by (II, 6.18).
227	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
228	% $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
229	% Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
230	% $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
231	% (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
232	% does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$). Now the Euler characteristic is
233	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
234	% = 1$, so we have
235	% $\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1$.
236	% On the other hand, $\deg D + P = \deg D + 1$, so our formula is true
237	% for $D$ if and only if it is true for $D + P$, as required."
238	\begin{proof}
239	  \leanok
240	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
241	        thm:divisor_degree_hom, def:divisor_closed_point}
242	  The proof is by induction on the structure of $\Div(C)$ as the free
243	  abelian group on closed points (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles},
244	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}). Both sides of the asserted
245	  equation are additive in $D$ (the right-hand side by linearity of
246	  $\deg$, \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}; the left-hand side by the
247	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
248	  for $D = 0$ and to show that it transports across the elementary
249	  modification $D \mapsto D + [P]$ at any closed point $P \in C$.
250	
251	  \emph{Base case $D = 0$.} The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(0)$ is the
252	  structure sheaf $\mathcal O_C$, and the formula
253	  $\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g$ reads
254	  \[
255	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
256	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
257	    \;=\; 1 - g.
258	  \]
259	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
260	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g$ (\Cref{def:genus}). The
261	  first term is $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$: $C$ is a
262	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
263	  closed field $\bar k$, so its connected components agree with its
264	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
265	  \cong \bar k$ as $\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
266	  statement that ``$H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k$ for any projective
267	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
268	  the project's $H^0$-bridge in
269	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
270	  two values gives $1 - g = 1 - g$, as required.
271	
272	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``$D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P$''
273	  step).} Let $P \in C$ be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
274	  argument: view $P$ as a closed subscheme of $C$; its structure
275	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$ supported at $P$,
276	  and its sheaf of ideals is $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C$
277	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out $P$ in $C$). Hence
278	  the short exact sequence
279	  \[
280	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
281	  \]
282	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
283	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (a locally free
284	  $\mathcal O_C$-module of rank $1$, so tensoring preserves exactness
285	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged) yields
286	  \[
287	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
288	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
289	    \to k(P) \to 0.
290	  \]
291	  Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence
292	  of coherent sheaves (Mathlib's preadditive-category Euler
293	  characteristic; see the Lean reference note at the end of this
294	  proof) gives
295	  \[
296	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
297	    \;=\;
298	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
299	  \]
300	  The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ has
301	  $H^0(C, k(P)) \cong \bar k$ and $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$ (the cohomology
302	  of a flasque sheaf supported at a closed point is concentrated in
303	  degree $0$, with $H^0$ equal to the stalk $\bar k$), so
304	  $\chi(k(P)) = 1$. Therefore
305	  \[
306	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
307	  \]
308	  On the other hand, $\deg([P]) = 1$ (every closed point contributes
309	  $1$ to the bare-sum degree of \Cref{def:divisor_degree}), so by
310	  \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
311	  \[
312	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
313	  \]
314	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
315	  $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ holds for $D$ if and only
316	  if it holds for $D + [P]$. Since the closed-point divisors $[P]$
317	  generate $\Div(C)$ as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
318	  from $D = 0$ to every $D \in \Div(C)$ by induction on the (signed)
319	  number of generators in the expression of $D$.
320	
321	  \emph{Lean reference note on $\chi$-additivity.} Mathlib (snapshot
322	  \texttt{b80f227}) provides the Euler-characteristic API
323	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
324	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar}, \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar})
325	  but does \emph{not} ship the specific additivity statement
326	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} [expected,
327	  not in Mathlib under that name]. The genus-$0$ proof of
328	  \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} therefore threads additivity of
329	  $\chi$ on a short exact sequence either as a project-side lemma
330	  proved inside \texttt{RRFormula.lean} (from
331	  \texttt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank} combined with
332	  the long exact sequence of $H^*$) or as a candidate Mathlib upstream
333	  PR. The base case $\chi(k(P)) = 1$ is closed by the Mathlib
334	  skyscraper-sheaf API together with the project's $H^0$-bridge
335	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}).
336	\end{proof}
337	
338	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
339	
340	\begin{theorem}
341	
342	
343	\leanok
344	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
345	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
347	  \uses{def:l_invariant, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
348	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
349	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
350	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-$0$ specialisation
351	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
352	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
353	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
354	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
355	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
356	  % $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
357	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$.
358	  % We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so
359	  % conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two
360	  % distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
361	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg K - D = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have
362	  % $l(K - D) = 0$, and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of
363	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim
364	  % Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
365	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
366	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
367	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $D \in \Div(C)$ with
368	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$. Then
369	  \[
370	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
371	  \]
372	\end{theorem}
373	
374	\begin{proof}
375	  \leanok
376	  \uses{def:l_invariant, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
377	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
378	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
379	  Specialising
380	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} to the genus-$0$
381	  hypothesis $g = 0$ yields
382	  \[
383	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
384	  \]
385	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} and the definition
386	  $\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
387	  (\Cref{def:l_invariant}), this rewrites as
388	  \[
389	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
390	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
391	  \]
392	  Under the standing genus-$0$ hypothesis and the assumption
393	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$, the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
394	  \[
395	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
396	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
397	  \]
398	  Substituting yields $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$, as required.
399	
400	  \emph{The $H^1$-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
401	  irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, the vanishing
402	  $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$ is the Hartshorne
403	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: $\ell(D) \neq 0$ forces
404	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$; equivalently, for a curve where every
405	  degree-$0$-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
406	  sections after a single point-twist, $H^1$ of the twist must vanish
407	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
408	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this $H^1$
409	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
410	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
411	  bundle $\mathcal O_C(D)$ and its cohomology are constructed; once
412	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
413	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
414	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
415	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
416	  $H^1$-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
417	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
418	  follow-up signature simplification.
419	
420	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
421	  this theorem is the genus-$0$ classification
422	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
423	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$
424	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
425	  (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
426	  produces a degree-$1$ morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via
427	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
428	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$, which is then upgraded to an
429	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
430	\end{proof}
431	
432	\section{Out of scope}
433	
434	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
435	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
436	
437	\begin{itemize}
438	  \item The general-$g$ Riemann--Roch theorem
439	    $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
440	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor $K$, the
441	    canonical sheaf $\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C$, and
442	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
443	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
444	    the genus-$0$ critical path does not require them; the general
445	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
446	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$
447	    from a Weil divisor $D$, the linear-equivalence isomorphism
448	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when $D \sim D'$, and
449	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
450	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
451	    $t$ exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
452	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
453	    of \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} the dependency on
454	    $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is reduced to its $H^0$ functor and the short
455	    exact sequence of the closed point.
456	  \item The vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0$ for
457	    $\deg(D) \geq 0$ on a genus-$0$ curve is consumed as a named
458	    premise by \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}; its closure is
459	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
460	    $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)$ is computed) and is recovered for
461	    a general smooth proper genus-$0$ curve via the isomorphism
462	    $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
463	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
464	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
465	    curve over $\bar k$ with $g = 0$ is isomorphic to
466	    $\mathbb P^1$'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
467	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
468	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
469	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
470	    \deg(D) + 1$ identity); the other input is the degree-$0$
471	    principal-divisor identity \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} from
472	    \texttt{RR.1}.
473	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping ($\chi$ as an alternating sum
474	    over all $i \geq 0$, the Grothendieck vanishing
475	    $H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0$ for $i > \dim C = 1$) is not separately
476	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
477	    $\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1$ which is the standing
478	    curve specialisation.
479	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ of a Weil divisor (RR.2$_\ast$)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of $\mathscr L(D)$; Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf $\mathcal O_X(D)$ from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
29	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \Div(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor $D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]$ to its associated invertible
39	sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ on $C$. The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	$\chi$-identity proof of \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor $D$ via the
51	``$D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]$'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$, which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much $\chi$-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}) constructs
70	the line bundle $\mathcal O_C([P])$ associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	$P \in C$ directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	\Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(D)$ at $D = [P]$ as canonically
74	isomorphic to \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via the
77	$\chi$-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of $C$ both reference $\mathcal O_C(D)$ for general $D$ (not
84	just $D = [P]$): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	$D \in \Div(C)$ and reads off $\deg(D)$ from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	\Cref{def:sheafOf}).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
92	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group $\Div(C)$ is the one defined in
103	\Cref{def:codim1_cycles} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is \Cref{def:prime_divisor}; the order
105	$\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor $Q$ is \Cref{def:order_at_point}; the
106	principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a non-zero rational function is
107	\Cref{def:principal_divisor}; the closed-point divisor $[P]$ is
108	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, and the present chapter's
111	\Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} makes the comparison
112	$\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}$ explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation $\mathcal O_C([P])$:
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on $\mathbf{SheafOfModules}$ (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData $\to$ SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no $\mathbf{SheafOfModules}$ dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-$\mathcal K_C$} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf $D$} as well, and \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} verifies
127	that the general construction at $D = [P]$ recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-$\mathcal K_C$
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	$\mathscr L(D)$) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general $D$ and at the $D = [P]$
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	$\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}$ a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
135	
136	\section{The invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$ of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor, def:order_at_point,
143	        def:principal_divisor}
144	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
145	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
146	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
147	  % (Cartier $\leftrightarrow$ Weil correspondence on an integral
148	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of $\mathscr L(D)$ as a
149	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field $K$). Read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
151	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
152	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
153	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
154	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
155	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
156	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
157	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
158	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, so $f_i^{-1}$ and
159	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
160	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
161	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If $X$ is an integral
162	  % scheme, the homomorphism $\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X$ of
163	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
164	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of $\mathscr K$, which
165	  % in this case is the constant sheaf $K$, where $K$ is the function
166	  % field of $X$."
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
168	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on $X$ and
169	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes $Y$, i.e., subschemes
170	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
171	  % Note also that if $X$ is an integral separated noetherian locally
172	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
173	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
174	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
175	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
176	  $\mathscr L(D)$), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-$K$
177	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
178	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
179	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
180	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
181	  $\bar k$ and let $D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor
182	  on $C$, with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors $Q$ of $C$ and
183	  supported on a finite set $\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}$
184	  (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}). The \emph{invertible sheaf
185	  $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to $D$}, also written
186	  $\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)$ in Hartshorne's notation, is the
187	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the constant sheaf
188	  $\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)$ (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
189	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
190	  field) defined section-wise by
191	  \[
192	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
193	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
194	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
195	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
196	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
197	  \]
198	  for each open $U \subseteq C$. Restriction along $V \hookrightarrow U$
199	  is the identity on the underlying carrier $K(C)$, which preserves
200	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
201	  the constraint set on $U$ embeds into the constraint set on $V$). The
202	  $\bar k$-module structure on
203	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ is restricted from the
204	  $\bar k$-module structure on $K(C)$ (a $\bar k$-scalar multiple of a
205	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
206	
207	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
208	  formal sum $D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]$ packages, on a curve where prime
209	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
210	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness $+$ dimension~$1$), the Cartier-divisor
211	  data $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
212	  open $U_i$ containing exactly one prime divisor $Q$ in the support of
213	  $D$, pick a uniformiser $\pi_Q \in K(C)$ at $Q$ and set
214	  $f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}$; on opens disjoint from the support of $D$, set
215	  $f_i := 1$. The transitions on overlaps $U_i \cap U_j$ are units in
216	  $\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}$ for each prime $Q$ in the overlap (any
217	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
218	  $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
219	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original $D$. The
220	  ``$f_i^{-1}$-generated'' subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$ from Hartshorne's
221	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
222	  the local generator $f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}$ has order $-n_Q$ at $Q$,
223	  so the local sections are exactly $f \in K(C)$ with
224	  $\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q$, on
225	  $U_i$.
226	
227	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
228	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} of \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP})
229	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
230	  \mathcal K_C$ is an invertible sheaf on $C$, independently of the
231	  choice of uniformisers $\pi_Q$ (any two choices differ by a unit, so
232	  they generate the same $\mathcal O_C$-submodule).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
235	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
236	  divisor $D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}$ and returns a
237	  $\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$ on
238	  $C.\text{left.toTopCat}$, where
239	  $J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}$ is the
240	  standard topology on the topological space underlying $C$. The
241	  closed-point specialisation $\mathcal O_C([P])$ is
242	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} of
243	  \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}; their agreement at $D = [P]$ is
244	  \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint} below. The construction follows
245	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
246	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
247	  $\bar k$-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
248	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
249	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
250	\end{definition}
251	
252	\section{Immediate corollaries}
253	
254	\begin{lemma}\leanok
255	[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
256	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
257	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
258	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles}
259	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
260	  $\bar k$. Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
261	  $0 \in \Div(C)$ is canonically the structure sheaf:
262	  \[
263	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
264	  \]
265	  as sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$, equivalently as
266	  objects in $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
267	\end{lemma}
268	
269	\begin{proof}
270	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
271	  At $D = 0$ the coefficient $n_Q = 0$ at every prime divisor $Q$, so the
272	  section condition of \Cref{def:sheafOf} reduces, on every open
273	  $U \subseteq C$, to
274	  \[
275	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
276	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
277	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
278	  \]
279	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
280	  scheme $C$ (the regular functions on $U$ are exactly the rational
281	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
282	  $C$ landing in $U$; this is the standard ``non-negative order $=$
283	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
284	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
285	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$. The restriction maps on both sides are
286	  the identity on $K(C)$, so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
287	  equality of sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$.
288	\end{proof}
289	
290	\begin{lemma}\leanok
291	[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
292	\Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}]
293	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
295	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
296	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles}
297	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
298	  $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
299	  $[P] \in \Div(C)$ via \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. Then the
300	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ of \Cref{def:sheafOf} agrees on
301	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
302	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ of \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}:
303	  \[
304	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
305	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
306	  \]
307	  as sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$, equivalently as
308	  objects in $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
309	\end{lemma}
310	
311	\begin{proof}
312	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
313	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point}
314	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
315	  $[P]$ has coefficients $n_Q = 1$ if $Q = P$ and $n_Q = 0$ otherwise
316	  (where $P$ is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
317	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}). Thus
318	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ contains $0$ and those
319	  $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ satisfying $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$ for every prime
320	  divisor $Q \in U$ with $Q \neq P$, plus $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$ if
321	  $P \in U$.
322	
323	  On the \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint} side, the same conditions
324	  are exactly the ones extracted by
325	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} of
326	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open $U$ (not only at the global section
327	  $U = C$): a rational function $f \in K(C)$ is a section of
328	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ on $U$ iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
329	  $Q \in U \setminus \{P\}$ and has at most a simple pole at $P$ when
330	  $P \in U$.
331	
332	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on $K(C)$. The two
333	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$ are therefore equal
334	  section-by-section as subsets of $K(C)$, and the equality propagates
335	  to an equality of sheaves of $\mathcal O_C$-modules. The
336	  $\bar k$-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
337	  $\bar k$-module structure on $K(C)$, so the identification is
338	  $\bar k$-linear on each section.
339	\end{proof}
340	
341	\begin{lemma}\leanok
342	[Short exact sequence for $D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]$]
343	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
345	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
346	        def:divisor_closed_point}
347	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
348	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any
349	  % point...''). Read from
350	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
351	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
352	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
353	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
354	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
355	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
356	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
357	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
358	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
359	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$.
360	  % (Since $\mathscr L(D + P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
361	  % it does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$.)"
362	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
363	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
364	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
365	  $\bar k$, let $D \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor, and let $P \in C$ be
366	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
367	  \Cref{def:prime_divisor} on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
368	  $\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ of
369	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-modules of $\mathcal K_C$ fits into a short exact
370	  sequence
371	  \[
372	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
373	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
374	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
375	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
376	  \]
377	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ (equivalently, in
378	  $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$), where $k(P)$ denotes the
379	  skyscraper sheaf at $P$ with stalk $\bar k$ (the residue field of $P$
380	  is $\bar k$ since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $P$ is a
381	  $\bar k$-rational closed point).
382	\end{lemma}
383	
384	\begin{proof}
385	  \uses{def:sheafOf, def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
386	        lem:sheafOf_singlePoint, def:order_at_point}
387	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
388	  proceeds in two beats:
389	
390	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at $P$).} The closed-subscheme
391	  inclusion $P \hookrightarrow C$ has structure sheaf
392	  $\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)$ (a skyscraper at $P$ with stalk $\bar k$),
393	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
394	  $\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])$ (the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
395	  $\mathcal K_C$ generated by a local uniformiser $\pi_P$ at $P$ and by
396	  $1$ away from $P$, i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
397	  principal closed subscheme $P$). This produces the structural short
398	  exact sequence
399	  \[
400	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
401	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
402	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
403	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
404	  \]
405	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$.
406	
407	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$).} The line bundle
408	  $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is locally free of rank~$1$
409	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), \Cref{def:sheafOf}), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
410	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is exact on $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
411	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
412	  \[
413	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
414	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
415	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
416	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
417	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
418	  \]
419	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
420	  \begin{itemize}
421	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
422	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
423	      $\Div(C)$-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
424	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
425	      ring-theoretic identity
426	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
427	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
428	      rational functions, where $n_Q^{D}$ is the coefficient of $Q$
429	      in $D$);
430	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
431	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
432	    \item $k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)$ (tensoring a
433	      skyscraper at $P$ by a locally free rank-$1$ sheaf preserves the
434	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over $P$ the line bundle
435	      $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P$ is a free $\mathcal O_{C,P}$-module of
436	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
437	      \cong k(P)$ is just $k(P)$; away from $P$ the skyscraper is the
438	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
439	  \end{itemize}
440	  Substituting yields
441	  \[
442	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
443	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
444	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
445	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
446	  \]
447	  exact in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$, as required.
448	
449	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
450	  $\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ is the
451	  inclusion of subsheaves of $\mathcal K_C$: at the level of sections,
452	  the constraint $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}$ of
453	  $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ implies the weaker constraint
454	  $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1$ of $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))$
455	  when $P \in U$ (constraints on $Q \neq P$ are unchanged), and the map
456	  is identity on $K(C)$ at the carrier level. The second map
457	  $\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)$ is the evaluation
458	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at $P$'' (i.e.\ the
459	  $\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}$-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of $f$
460	  at $P$), which is well-defined on $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))$
461	  modulo the subgroup of sections with $\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}$ ---
462	  i.e.\ modulo $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$.
463	\end{proof}
464	
465	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
466	
467	The construction of \Cref{def:sheafOf} is presheaf-by-hand: a
468	per-open $\bar k$-submodule of $K(C)$, with restriction along
469	$V \hookrightarrow U$ given by the identity on $K(C)$. This section
470	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
471	target category $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$.
472	
473	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For $V \subseteq U$, the
474	constraint set defining $\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ is a \emph{subset}
475	of the constraint set defining $\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))$ (fewer
476	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on $K(C)$ restricts
477	to a well-defined inclusion
478	$\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))$.
479	Composition $W \subseteq V \subseteq U$ is again identity-on-$K(C)$, so
480	strict functoriality holds.
481	
482	\paragraph{$\bar k$-linearity.} A $\bar k$-scalar $\lambda \in \bar k$
483	acts on $K(C)$ by multiplication, and
484	$\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)$ for $\lambda \neq 0$
485	(units have order zero); for $\lambda = 0$, $\lambda \cdot f = 0$ is
486	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
487	$\bar k$-submodule and the restriction maps are $\bar k$-linear.
488	
489	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
490	``$\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q$ for every prime divisor $Q \in U$'' is
491	\emph{stalk-local} at each $Q$: it depends only on the germ of $f$ at
492	the point of $C$ underlying $Q$, equivalently on the image of $f$ in
493	the DVR $\mathcal O_{C, Q}$. Consequently the sheaf property reduces
494	to two ingredients:
495	\begin{enumerate}
496	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is a
497	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at $K(C)$.} The constant
498	    presheaf at $K(C)$ is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
499	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
500	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
501	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
502	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
503	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
504	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
505	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
506	    prime divisor.
507	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
508	    $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$ to
509	    $\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})$.} The categorical forget
510	    $\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}$ creates limits, hence
511	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of $\bar k$-modules is a
512	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
513	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
514	    $\bar k$-module-valued $\mathcal O_C(D)$.
515	\end{enumerate}
516	
517	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open $U \subseteq C$ disjoint from
518	the support of $D$, the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
519	constraint $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$ for every prime divisor $Q \in U$; hence
520	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U$, and the trivialisation
521	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U$ is the identity. On a small
522	open $U \subseteq C$ containing exactly one prime divisor $Q$ from the
523	support of $D$, the local generator $\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)$ trivialises
524	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U$ as a free rank-$1$ $\mathcal O_C|_U$-module: a
525	section $f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))$ satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
526	-n_Q$ iff $\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
527	``multiplication by $\pi_Q^{n_Q}$'' map is an
528	$\mathcal O_C|_U$-linear isomorphism
529	$\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U$. The two types
530	of trivialising opens cover $C$ (any prime divisor lies in at most
531	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of $D$ is
532	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
533	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
534	the cocycle is in $\mathcal O_C^{\times}$ and the sheaf is invertible.
535	
536	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
537	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
538	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
539	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
540	landed the analogous bottom-up
541	$\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})$-valued construction for the
542	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
543	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
544	\texttt{RR.1} (\Cref{def:order_at_point} and surrounding lemmas).
545

585	\section{Out of scope}
586	
587	\begin{itemize}
588	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
589	    isomorphism $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when
590	    $D \sim D'$ (i.e.\ $D - D' = \div(f)$ for some
591	    $f \in K(C)^{\times}$) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
592	    It is not needed for the genus-$0$ critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
593	    consumes only $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))$ and the SES additivity at
594	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
595	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
596	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in $\Div(C)$ rather than
597	    in $\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \Div(C) / \sim$. The
598	    Cartier-divisor $\leftrightarrow$ \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
599	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
600	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
601	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
602	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
603	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
604	    \Cref{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add} at the single instance
605	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
606	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
607	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
608	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
609	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
610	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
611	  \item \textbf{Pullback of $\mathcal O_C(D)$ along a morphism.} The
612	    pullback $f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)$ for
613	    a morphism of curves $f : C' \to C$, used in degree-computation
614	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
615	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-$0$ critical path.
616	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
617	    space.} The identification
618	    $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)$ for a general Weil divisor
619	    $D$ (i.e.\ the analogue of
620	    \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} for general
621	    $D$) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
622	    case $D = [P]$ is the only specialisation consumed by the
623	    genus-$0$ critical path, and it is handled in
624	    \texttt{RR.3} via \Cref{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}.
625	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
626	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
627	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme
628	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ critical path
629	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
630	    does not engage them.
631	\end{itemize}

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface $S$ over a field --- such as $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$
28	or the product $C \times C$ of a smooth projective curve $C$ with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension $2$. To extend a rational map
30	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from $S$ to an abelian variety across a codimension-$2$ closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} $2$, hence a rational function with no codimension-$1$ poles on a neighbourhood of $x$
33	extends across $x$. The depth $\geq 2$ inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring $R$ one has $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$,
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension $d$ admits an $R$-regular sequence of length $d$ generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$, i.e.\ $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its $\text{Ext}$ reformulation
47	    (Definition~\ref{def:depth} and Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~\ref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~\ref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let $R$ be a ring, and $I \subset R$ an ideal. Let $M$ be a finite $R$-module.
74	  %  The {\it $I$-depth} of $M$, denoted $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if $IM \not = M$, then $\text{depth}_I(M)$ is the supremum in
77	  %  $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences in $I$,
78	  %  \item if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$.
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If $(R, \mathfrak m)$ is local we call $\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)$ simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of $M$."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring, let $I \subseteq R$ be an ideal, and let $M$ be a finitely
84	  generated $R$-module. The \emph{$I$-depth} of $M$, written $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is the
85	  supremum in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences contained
86	  in $I$, provided $IM \neq M$; if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$. When
87	  $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  $\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)$ and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of $M$. The depth of the ring $R$ itself is $\text{depth}(R)$, the depth
90	  of $R$ as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence $f_1, \dots, f_r \in R$ is called \emph{$M$-regular} if
94	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0$ and for each $i$, the element $f_i$ is a non-zero-divisor on
95	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M$. The empty sequence is regular on $M$ iff $M \neq 0$ (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention $\text{depth}(0) = \infty$
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the $\text{Ext}$ characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via $\text{Ext}$]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  \uses{def:depth}
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let $R$ be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$.
114	  %  Let $M$ be a nonzero finite $R$-module. Then $\text{depth}(M)$
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer $i$ such that
116	  %  $\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)$ is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  $\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}$ and let $M$ be a nonzero finitely generated $R$-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let $\delta(M)$ denote the depth of $M$ and let $i(M)$ denote
127	%  the smallest integer $i$ such that $\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)$
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that $i(M) < \infty$.
129	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-ideal-nonzerodivisor} we have
130	%  $\delta(M) = 0$ if and only if $i(M) = 0$, because
131	%  $\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)$ exactly means
132	%  that $i(M) = 0$. Hence if $\delta(M)$ or $i(M)$ is $> 0$, then we may
133	%  choose $x \in \mathfrak m$ such that (a) $x$ is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on $M$, and (b) $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1$.
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  $0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-long-exact-seq-ext}:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since $x \in \mathfrak m$ all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma \ref{lemma-annihilate-ext}.
142	%  Thus it is clear that $i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1$. Induction on
143	%  $\delta(M)$ finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  \uses{def:depth}
146	  Write $\delta := \text{depth}(M)$ and $i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}$.
147	  The base case $\delta = 0$ is exactly $\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0$ (some element of
148	  $M$ is annihilated by $\mathfrak{m}$, i.e.\ $\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)$, i.e.\
149	  $\mathfrak{m}$ contains a zero-divisor on $M$), which is $i = 0$. For the inductive step
150	  pick $x \in \mathfrak{m}$ a non-zero-divisor on $M$ with
151	  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1$. The short exact sequence
152	  $0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0$ induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
153	  -)$. Multiplication by $x$ annihilates each $\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
154	  $x \in \mathfrak{m}$ and $\kappa$ is annihilated by $\mathfrak{m}$), so the long exact
155	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
156	  $\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ at the relevant
157	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by $1$. By induction
158	  $i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1$, so $i = \delta$.
159	\end{proof}

161	\section{Projective dimension}
162	\label{sec:ab_pd}
163	
164	\begin{definition}
165	
166	\leanok
167	  [Projective dimension]
168	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
169	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
170	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
171	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
172	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
173	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
174	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
175	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
176	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring and let $M$ be an $R$-module. The \emph{projective
177	  dimension} of $M$, written $\text{pd}_R(M)$, is the infimum in
178	  $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths $n$ of projective resolutions
179	  \[
180	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
181	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
182	  \]
183	  with each $P_i$ a projective $R$-module. We say $M$ has \emph{finite projective dimension}
184	  if $\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty$, and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
185	  convention $\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty$ (some references); we use $\text{pd}_R(0) = 0$ here
186	  (the empty resolution $0 \to 0$ of length $0$), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
187	  Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} concerns only nonzero $M$.
188	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated $R$-module of finite projective
189	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each $P_i$
190	  free of finite rank and each transition map $P_{i+1} \to P_i$ landing in $\mathfrak{m} P_i$.
191	\end{definition}
192	
193	The two basic facts about $\text{pd}_R(M)$ needed downstream are:
194	\begin{itemize}
195	  \item $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ iff $M$ is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
196	    iff $M$ is free);
197	  \item if $0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0$ is a short exact sequence with $P$ projective, then
198	    $\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1$ provided $K \neq 0$.
199	\end{itemize}
200	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under

200	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
201	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
202	
203	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
204	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
205	
206	\begin{lemma}
207	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
208	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
209	  \lean{RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact}
210	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
211	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
212	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
213	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
214	  % "Let $R$ be a local Noetherian ring. Let $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$
215	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite $R$-modules.
216	  %  \begin{enumerate}
217	  %  \item
218	  %  $\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}$
219	  %  \item
220	  %  $\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}$
221	  %  \item
222	  %  $\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}$
223	  %  \end{enumerate}"
224	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
225	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring and let
226	  $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$ be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
227	  $R$-modules. Then
228	  \begin{enumerate}
229	    \item $\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)$;
230	    \item $\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)$;
231	    \item $\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)$.
232	  \end{enumerate}
233	\end{lemma}
234	
235	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
236	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
237	%  $\Ext^i(\kappa, N)$, see Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-ext},
238	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
239	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
240	%  from Lemma \ref{lemma-long-exact-seq-ext}."
241	\begin{proof}
242	  \uses{lem:depth_via_ext}
243	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}, $\text{depth}(N')$ (resp.\ $\text{depth}(N)$,
244	  $\text{depth}(N'')$) is the smallest index at which $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)$ is
245	  nonzero on $N'$ (resp.\ $N$, $N''$). Apply the long exact $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)$
246	  sequence to $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$:
247	  \[
248	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
249	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
250	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
251	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
252	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
253	  \]
254	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
255	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
256	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
257	  the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}.
258	\end{proof}

260	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
261	\label{sec:ab_main}
262	
263	\begin{theorem}
264	
265	\leanok
266	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
267	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
268	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
269	  \uses{def:depth, def:projective_dimension, lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
270	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
271	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
272	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
273	  % "Let $R$ be a Noetherian local ring. Let $M$ be a nonzero finite $R$-module
274	  %  which has finite projective dimension $\text{pd}_R(M)$. Then we have
275	  %  $$
276	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
277	  %  $$"
278	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
279	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
280	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
281	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring and let $M$ be a nonzero finitely
282	  generated $R$-module of finite projective dimension. Then
283	  \[
284	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
285	  \]
286	\end{theorem}
287	
288	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
289	% "We prove this by induction on $\text{depth}(M)$. The most interesting
290	%  case is the case $\text{depth}(M) = 0$. In this case, let
291	%  $$
292	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
293	%  $$
294	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so $e = \text{pd}_R(M)$.
295	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-add-trivial-complex} we may assume all matrix
296	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
297	%  ideal of $R$. Then on the one hand, by
298	%  Proposition \ref{proposition-what-exact} we see that
299	%  $\text{depth}(R) \geq e$. On the other hand, breaking the long
300	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
301	%  \begin{align*}
302	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
303	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
304	%  \ldots,\\
305	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
306	%  \end{align*}
307	%  we see, using Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-in-ses}, that
308	%  \begin{align*}
309	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
310	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
311	%  \ldots,\\
312	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
313	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
314	%  \end{align*}
315	%  and since $\text{depth}(M) = 0$ we conclude $\text{depth}(R) \leq e$.
316	%  This finishes the proof of the case $\text{depth}(M) = 0$.
317	%
318	%  Induction step. If $\text{depth}(M) > 0$, then we pick $x \in \mathfrak m$
319	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both $M$ and $R$. This is possible, because
320	%  either $\text{pd}_R(M) > 0$ and $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ by the aforementioned
321	%  Proposition \ref{proposition-what-exact} or $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ in which
322	%  case $M$ is finite free hence also $\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0$.
323	%  Thus $\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-in-ses}
324	%  (for example) and we can find an $x \in \mathfrak m$ which is a nonzerodivisor
325	%  on both $R$ and $M$. Let
326	%  $$
327	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
328	%  $$
329	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
330	%  shows that
331	%  $$
332	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
333	%  M/xM \to 0
334	%  $$
335	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus $\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$.
336	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-drops-by-one} we have
337	%  $\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$ and
338	%  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1$.
339	%  Till now depths have all been depths as $R$ modules, but we observe that
340	%  $\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$ and similarly for $R/xR$.
341	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
342	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for $M/xM$ over $R/xR$. Since the
343	%  depths of both $R/xR$ and $M/xM$ have decreased by one and the projective
344	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
345	\begin{proof}
346	  \uses{def:depth, def:projective_dimension, lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
347	  We induct on $\text{depth}(M)$.
348	
349	  \emph{Base case $\text{depth}(M) = 0$.} Let $e := \text{pd}_R(M)$ and pick a minimal
350	  finite free resolution
351	  \[
352	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
353	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
354	  \]
355	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in $\mathfrak{m}$ (this is the
356	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
357	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
358	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
359	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of $r$-minors) gives
360	  $\text{depth}(R) \geq e$. On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
361	  sequences
362	  \[
363	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
364	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
365	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
366	  \]
367	  and applying Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
368	  yields the chain of inequalities
369	  $\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1$,
370	  $\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2$, \dots,
371	  $\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)$, and finally
372	  $\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e$. Since $\text{depth}(M) = 0$ we deduce
373	  $\text{depth}(R) \leq e$. Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
374	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
375	
376	  \emph{Inductive step $\text{depth}(M) > 0$.} We claim there exists $x \in \mathfrak{m}$
377	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both $R$ and $M$. Indeed: either $\text{pd}_R(M) > 0$, in
378	  which case $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
379	  of a minimal resolution, or $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ in which case $M$ is finite free and
380	  $\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0$. Either way both $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ and
381	  $\text{depth}(M) > 0$, hence $\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0$ by
382	  Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
383	  \mathfrak{m}$ exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of $M$ as above; the snake
384	  lemma applied to multiplication by $x$ gives a minimal finite free resolution
385	  \[
386	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
387	  \]
388	  of $M/xM$ over $R/xR$, of the same length $e$. So
389	  $\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)$. By Stacks
390	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both $\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$ and
391	  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1$, and one verifies
392	  $\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$ (regular sequences in $\mathfrak{m}$
393	  correspond to regular sequences in $\mathfrak{m}/(x)$). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
394	  $M/xM$ over $R/xR$:
395	  \[
396	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
397	  \]
398	  Substituting yields
399	  $\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$, i.e.\
400	  $\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)$, as required.
401	\end{proof}

403	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
404	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
405	
406	\begin{definition}
407	
408	\leanok
409	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
410	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
411	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
412	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
413	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "A Noetherian local ring $R$ is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
416	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
417	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
418	  A Noetherian local ring $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
419	  $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$ (equivalently, if $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
420	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite $R$-module $M$ means
421	  $\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)$, cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
422	\end{definition}
423	
424	\begin{corollary}
425	
426	\leanok
427	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
428	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
429	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
430	  \uses{def:depth, def:cohen_macaulay_local, thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
431	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
432	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
433	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
434	  % "Let $R$ be a regular local ring and let
435	  %  $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ be a minimal set of generators
436	  %  for the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$. Then
437	  %  $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ is a regular sequence, and
438	  %  each $R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)$ is a regular local ring
439	  %  of dimension $d - c$. In particular $R$ is Cohen-Macaulay."
440	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
441	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is
442	  regular local of Krull dimension $d$, then $\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)$.
443	\end{corollary}
444	
445	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
446	% "Note that $R/x_1R$ is a Noetherian local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$
447	%  by Lemma \ref{lemma-one-equation} with $x_2, \ldots, x_d$
448	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
449	%  Since $R$ is a domain by Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-domain}
450	%  $x_1$ is a nonzerodivisor."
451	\begin{proof}
452	  \uses{def:depth, def:cohen_macaulay_local}
453	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be regular of dimension $d$ and pick a minimal generating set
454	  $x_1, \dots, x_d$ of $\mathfrak{m}$. By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), $R$ is
455	  an integral domain, so $x_1$ is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient $R/x_1 R$ is a Noetherian
456	  local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$ (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
457	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by $x_2, \dots, x_d$, hence
458	  has dimension exactly $d - 1$ and is again regular of dimension $d - 1$. Inducting on $d$,
459	  $x_1, \dots, x_d$ is an $R$-regular sequence, so
460	  $\text{depth}(R) \geq d$. The reverse inequality $\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d$
461	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
462	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus $\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)$, i.e.\
463	  $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay.
464	
465	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
466	  Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take $M := R$ itself. Then $M$ is
467	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank $1$), so $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$. The
468	  formula gives $\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)$, a tautology. The non-tautological
469	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension $d$, every
470	  finitely generated module $M$ has $\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d$ (Stacks tag 00O2,
471	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an $M$ then
472	  relates $\text{depth}(M)$ to $d - \text{pd}_R(M)$, which is the form most often used in
473	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
474	  $R$ Cohen--Macaulay itself.
475	\end{proof}
476	
477	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
478	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
479	
480	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
481	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from a regular projective surface $S$ to an abelian variety $A$
482	across a closed point $x \in S$ that is missing from the domain of definition $U \subseteq S$.
483	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
484	
485	\begin{enumerate}
486	  \item The local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
487	    dimension $2$ (because $S$ is regular and $\dim S = 2$).
488	  \item By Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
489	    $\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2$. Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
490	    $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$-regular sequence of length $2$ (any two transverse generators).
491	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_S$ on $U = S \setminus \{x\}$ extend
492	    uniquely across $x$ because $H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ and
493	    $H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
494	    $\geq 2$ at $x$ by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
495	  \item For a rational map $f : S \dashrightarrow A$ with codimension-$\geq 2$ indeterminacy
496	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-$\geq 2$ input produces a sheaf-theoretic
497	    extension of $f$ to all of $S$: pull back any sheaf $\mathcal{O}_A$-affine-locally,
498	    obtain sections over $U$, and extend across $x$ by the above.
499	\end{enumerate}
500	
501	So Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
502	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
503	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
504	Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} are formalized in
505	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
506	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
507	
508	\section{Lean encoding}
509	\label{sec:ab_lean_encoding}
510	
511	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}.
512	Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) already exposes substantial parts
513	of the depth / projective-dimension API:
