43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ throughout. By a \emph{variety} over $\bar k$
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over $\bar k$
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over $\bar k$.
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to Y$ defined on a dense open subset
54	$U \subseteq X$, where two pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$, $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ are equivalent
55	if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$ (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map $f$
56	is \emph{defined at} a point $x \in X$ if there is some representative $(U, \varphi_U)$
57	with $x \in U$; the set of such $x$ is an open subset $\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X$,
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of $f$.
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of $X$?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on $X$ or $Y$ the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if $Y$ is complete (proper over $\bar k$), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-$1$ point of a normal source $X$ (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if $Y$ is a group variety, the difference map
75	    $\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}$ on $X \times X$ has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For $Y$ an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-$1$ extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's $\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}$ API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let $V$ and $W$
99	  % be varieties over $k$\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map $\varphi$ is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % $v$ of $V$ if $v \in U$ for some $(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi$. The set $U_1$ of
103	  % $v$ at which $\varphi$ is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % $\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W$ such that $(U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi$ --- clearly,
105	  % $U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U$ and we can define $\varphi_1$ to be
106	  % the regular map such that $\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U$ for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map between varieties over $\bar k$.
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of $f$ is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of $f$. The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of $f$ is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map $f$ is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of $X$) if and only if $Z(f) = \emptyset$.
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of $Z(f)$ is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus}
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset $U$ of $V$ whose complement $V \smallsetminus U$ has codimension $\geq 2$"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map $\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W$ from a normal
145	  % variety $V$ to a complete variety $W$ is defined on an open subset $U$ of $V$ whose
146	  % complement $V \smallsetminus U$ has codimension $\geq 2$."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map between varieties over $\bar k$.
150	  We say $f$ is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-$\geq 2$
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset $Z(f) \subseteq X$
152	  has codimension $\geq 2$ in $X$. Equivalently, no prime divisor of $X$ is contained in
153	  $Z(f)$; equivalently, every point $\eta \in X$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$
154	  lies in $\mathrm{Dom}(f)$.
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of $Z(f)$. Phrasing the codim-$\geq 2$ condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  ($Z(f)$ has codim $\geq 2$ from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} $Z(f)$ is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} $\Rightarrow$ $Z(f)$
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: $f$ is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  $\eta \in X$ with $\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1$, $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}$. The encoding matches the standing $\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of $X$ and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-$1$ indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}

176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-$1$ extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	
186	\leanok
187	  [Smooth $\Rightarrow$ DVR at every codim-$1$ point]
188	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
190	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
191	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
192	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
193	  % PDF page 147).
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme $X$ is \emph{regular in
195	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
196	  % local ring $\mathcal O_x$ of $X$ of dimension one is regular."
197	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
198	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
199	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
200	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of

200	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
201	  % closed points."
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its generic
203	  % point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at $\eta$ is a discrete
204	  % valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the function field of $X$."
205	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
206	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
207	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$ and let $\eta \in X$ be a point with
208	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a
209	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field $K(X)$ of $X$.
210	\end{lemma}
211	
212	\begin{proof}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  By smoothness, $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is regular for every closed point $x \in X$
216	  (Hartshorne I.5.1). Localising a regular local ring at a prime ideal yields a regular
217	  local ring; in particular, for $\eta \in X$ with
218	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is regular
219	  of Krull dimension $1$. A regular local ring of Krull dimension $1$ is exactly a
220	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
221	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field $K(X)$ of $X$ by
222	  irreducibility of $X$ (the unique generic point of $X$ lies above $\eta$, and the
223	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
224	
225	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
226	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
227	  instance/lemma stating that $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a Mathlib
228	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
229	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
230	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta$ = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
231	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
232	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
233	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
234	  $\Leftrightarrow$ DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
235	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
236	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
237	\end{proof}

239	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-$\geq 2$ extension}
240	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
241	
242	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
243	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-$1$
244	point.
245	
246	\begin{theorem}
247	
248	\leanok
249	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-$\geq 2$ extension to a complete target]
250	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
252	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:codim_one_indeterminacy, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
253	        cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
254	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
255	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
256	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
257	  % \dashrightarrow W$ from a normal variety $V$ to a complete variety $W$ is defined
258	  % on an open subset $U$ of $V$ whose complement $V \smallsetminus U$ has codimension
259	  % $\geq 2$.}"
260	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
261	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$, let $Y$ be a complete variety over
262	  $\bar k$, and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map. Then the
263	  indeterminacy locus $Z(f)$ (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
264	  $\geq 2$ in $X$; equivalently, $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
265	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
266	
267	  Specialising further: if in addition $f$ \emph{is} codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
268	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of $X$ is contained in $Z(f)$), then
269	  $\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X$ and $f$ extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
270	  \to Y$. The extension is unique because $X$ is reduced and $\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
271	  in $X$.
272	\end{theorem}
273	
274	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that $V$ is a curve. Thus we are
275	% given a nonsingular curve $V$ and a regular map $\varphi \colon U \to W$ from an
276	% open subset of $V$ which we want to extend to $V$. Consider the maps
277	% [diagram: $U \hookrightarrow V$, $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
278	% \subseteq V \times W$, $U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
279	% $V \times W$ to $V$ and to $W$]
280	% Let $U'$ be the image of $U$ in $V \times W$, and let $Z$ be its closure. The image
281	% of $Z$ in $V$ is closed (because $W$ is complete), and contains $U$ (the composite
282	% $U \to V$ is the given inclusion), and so $Z$ maps onto $V$. The maps $U \to U'
283	% \to U$ are isomorphisms. Therefore, $Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
284	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
285	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
286	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map $V \times W \to W$
287	% to $Z\ (\simeq V)$ is the extension of $\varphi$ to $V$ we are seeking.
288	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
289	% $U$ be the largest subset on which $\varphi$ is defined, and suppose that $V
290	% \smallsetminus U$ has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor $Z$ in $V
291	% \smallsetminus U$. Because $V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
292	% valuation ring $\mathcal O_Z$ with field of fractions $k(V)$. The map $\varphi$
293	% defines a morphism of schemes $\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W$, which the
294	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
295	% morphism $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W$. This implies that $\varphi$
296	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets $Z$ in a nonempty set,
297	% which is a contradiction."
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:codim_one_indeterminacy, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
300	        cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
301	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
302	
303	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-$1$ components.} Suppose for contradiction that $Z(f)$
304	  contains a prime divisor $W \subseteq X$ (i.e.\ a codim-$1$ irreducible component).
305	  Let $\eta \in W$ be its generic point, so $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$. By
306	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
307	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field $K(X)$. Any representative
308	  $(U, \varphi_U) \in f$ has $U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset$ and so determines
309	  a $\bar k$-algebra map from the function field $K(Y)$ of $Y$ to $K(X)$: explicitly,
310	  $\varphi_U$ defines a morphism of schemes $\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y$ (since
311	  $\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))$ is the generic point of $X$). By the valuative criterion
312	  of properness applied to the complete variety $Y$
313	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
314	  extends to a morphism $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y$. Standard
315	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood $V$ of $\eta$ in
316	  $X$ on which $f$ has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
317	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)$, contradicting $\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)$. Hence no codim-$1$
318	  component of $X$ lies in $Z(f)$, i.e.\ $Z(f)$ has codimension $\geq 2$.
319	
320	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-$\geq 2$ points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
321	  $\Rightarrow$ extension" half).} Suppose now that $Z(f)$ has codimension $\geq 2$
322	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce $Z(f) = \emptyset$ under the additional input
323	  that ``$f$ is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
324	  point $x \in Z(f)$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2$. By
325	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
326	  $\mathcal O_{X, x}$, we have
327	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2$. The sheaf
328	  $\mathcal O_X$ on the punctured neighbourhood $V \smallsetminus \{x\}$
329	  (any small affine neighbourhood $V \ni x$) has $H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0$
330	  and $H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0$ (the depth-$\geq 2$ characterisation of
331	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
332	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of $Y$ around the
333	  closure $\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}$ along $f$; the resulting sections of
334	  $\mathcal O_X$ on $V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)$ extend uniquely to sections on $V$,
335	  yielding a regular representative of $f$ on $V$. Hence $x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)$,
336	  contradicting $x \in Z(f)$. Therefore $Z(f) = \emptyset$ and $f$ extends to a
337	  regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X \to Y$.
338	
339	  Uniqueness of $\widetilde f$ is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
340	  principle: two morphisms $X \to Y$ that agree on the dense open $\mathrm{Dom}(f)$
341	  agree on all of $X$.
342	\end{proof}
343	
344	\begin{remark}
345	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
346	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
347	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
348	  Step~2 uses the depth-$\geq 2$ extension property at a codim-$\geq 2$ regular
349	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
350	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension $d$
351	  $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay of depth $d$). For our application the source $X$ is a
352	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
353	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
354	
355	  Concretely, when $X$ is a smooth surface (e.g.\ $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ or
356	  $C \times C$ for a smooth projective curve $C$), the codim-$\geq 2$ closed points of
357	  $X$ are exactly the closed points of $X$ (because $\dim X = 2$), and at each such
358	  point $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is regular of dimension $2$, hence Cohen--Macaulay of
359	  depth $2$. The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
360	  symmetric-product rational map $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ along the Abel--Jacobi
361	  birational equivalence to $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$.
362	\end{remark}
363	
364	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
365	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
366	
367	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target $Y$ is a group
368	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension $1$.
369	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension $\geq 2$'' conclusion,
370	this forces the locus to be empty.
371	
372	\begin{lemma}
373	
374	\leanok
375	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-$1$ structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
376	  variety]
377	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
378	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
379	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles}
380	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
381	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
382	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let $\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G$
383	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
384	  % $\varphi$ is defined on all of $V$ or the points where it is not defined form a
385	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in $V$ (i.e., a finite union of prime
386	  % divisors).}"
387	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
388	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$, let $G$ be a group variety over
389	  $\bar k$ (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
390	  G$ be a rational map. Then either $f$ is defined on all of $X$, or the indeterminacy
391	  locus $Z(f) \subseteq X$ is a closed subset of pure codimension $1$ (i.e.\ a finite
392	  union of prime divisors).
393	
394	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that $f$ has a non-empty indeterminacy
395	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension $\geq 2$.
396	\end{lemma}
397	
398	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
399	%   $\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G$, $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
400	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
401	% More precisely, if $(U, \varphi_U)$ represents $\varphi$, then $\Phi$ is the rational
402	% map represented by
403	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
404	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
405	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
406	% Clearly $\Phi$ is defined at a diagonal point $(x, x)$ if $\varphi$ is defined at
407	% $x$, and then $\Phi(x, x) = e$. Conversely, if $\Phi$ is defined at $(x, x)$, then
408	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of $(x, x)$; in particular, there will be an
409	% open subset $U$ of $V$ such that $\Phi$ is defined on $\{x\} \times U$. After
410	% possible replacing $U$ by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing $x$),
411	% $\varphi$ will be defined on $U$. For $u \in U$, the formula
412	%   $\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)$
413	% defines $\varphi$ at $x$. Thus $\varphi$ is defined at $x$ if and only if $\Phi$ is
414	% defined at $(x, x)$. The rational map $\Phi$ defines a map
415	%   $\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)$.
416	% Since $\Phi$ sends $(x, x)$ to $e$ if it is defined there, it follows that $\Phi$
417	% is defined at $(x, x)$ if and only if
418	%   $\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}$.
419	% Now $V \times V$ is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
420	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function $f$ on $V \times V$, write
421	%   $\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty$,
422	% with $\operatorname{div}(f)_0$ and $\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty$ effective divisors
423	% --- note that $\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0$. Then
424	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
425	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
426	% Suppose $\phi$ is not defined at $x$. Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
427	% (\varphi^\ast)$, $(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty$, and clearly $\Phi$ is
428	% not defined at the points $(y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty$.
429	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in $\Delta$ (AG 9.2), and when we identify
430	% it with a subset of $V$, it is a subset of $V$ of codimension one passing through
431	% $x$ on which $\varphi$ is not defined."
432	\begin{proof}
433	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles,
434	        lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
435	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
436	  fix a representative $(U, \varphi_U)$ of $f$.
437	
438	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
439	  \[
440	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
441	    \qquad
442	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
443	  \]
444	  via the composition
445	  \[
446	    U \times U
447	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
448	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
449	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
450	  \]
451	  where $\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G$ is the group-variety inverse and
452	  $m \colon G \times G \to G$ is the group law. The map $\Phi$ is rational by
453	  composition: its domain is at least $U \times U \subseteq X \times X$, which is dense
454	  because $U$ is dense in $X$.
455	
456	  \emph{Sub-step 2: $\Phi$-defined at $(x, x)$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $\varphi$-defined at
457	  $x$.} If $\varphi$ is defined at $x$, the explicit formula $\Phi(x, x) = e$ (the
458	  identity of $G$) shows $\Phi$ is defined at $(x, x)$. Conversely, if $\Phi$ is
459	  defined at $(x, x)$, openness of $\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$ in $X \times X$ produces an
460	  open set $W \ni (x, x)$ on which $\Phi$ is defined; by projection (taking the slice
461	  $\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W$ for some open $W' \subseteq X$, possibly shrinking
462	  $W'$ so $\varphi$ is defined on $W'$ as well --- $W'$ need not contain $x$), the
463	  identity
464	  \[
465	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
466	    \qquad u \in W',
467	  \]
468	  exhibits a regular representative of $\varphi$ at $x$. Hence $x \in
469	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)$ if and only if $(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
470	
471	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map $\Phi$
472	  pulls $\mathcal O_{G, e}$ back to a $\bar k$-subalgebra of the function field
473	  $K(X \times X)$:
474	  \[
475	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
476	  \]
477	  Because $\Phi$ sends $(x, x)$ to $e$ wherever it is defined, $\Phi$ is defined at
478	  $(x, x)$ if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
479	  \times X, (x, x)}$ (i.e.\ every local function on $G$ near $e$ has no pole at
480	  $(x, x)$ after pullback).
481	
482	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension $1$.} The variety $X \times X$ is
483	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over $\bar k$), so the Weil-divisor
484	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
485	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on $X \times X$. For any nonzero rational function
486	  $f \in K(X \times X)^\ast$ decompose its divisor as
487	  \[
488	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
489	  \]
490	  with $\mathrm{div}(f)_0$ and $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ effective and
491	  $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0$ (Milne's notation, matching the
492	  project's $\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y$ of
493	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: $\mathrm{div}(f)_0$ collects the prime divisors with
494	  $\ord_Y(f) > 0$ and $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ those with $\ord_Y(f) < 0$). The local
495	  ring at $(x, x)$ can be characterised as
496	  \[
497	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
498	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
499	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
500	  \]
501	  Suppose $f \in K(X \times X)^\ast$ is not in $\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}$;
502	  equivalently, $(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)$. The pole divisor
503	  $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ is a finite sum of prime divisors of $X \times X$ (i.e.\ a
504	  closed subset of pure codimension $1$ in $X \times X$). Its intersection with the
505	  diagonal $\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X$ is a closed subset of pure codimension $1$
506	  in $\Delta_X$ (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
507	  divisor of $X \times X$ either contains $\Delta_X$ or meets it in pure codimension
508	  $1$; on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
509	  applied to the local equation of $\Delta_X$ inside $X \times X$).
510	
511	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose $f \colon X \dashrightarrow G$ is not defined on all of
512	  $X$. Pick $x \in Z(f)$. By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
513	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})$ with $(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
514	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
515	  \Delta_X$ is pure codimension $1$ in $\Delta_X$, passing through $(x, x)$.
516	  Identifying $\Delta_X$ with $X$ via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
517	  closed subset of $X$ has pure codimension $1$ and passes through $x$. By Sub-step~2,
518	  every point of this codim-$1$ subset is a point where $\Phi$ is undefined on the
519	  diagonal, hence where $\varphi$ is undefined. Thus $Z(\varphi)$ contains a pure
520	  codim-$1$ closed subset passing through $x$; since $x \in Z(\varphi)$ was arbitrary,
521	  every irreducible component of $Z(\varphi)$ has codimension exactly $1$, i.e.\
522	  $Z(\varphi)$ is of pure codimension $1$.
523	\end{proof}
524	
525	\begin{remark}
526	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
527	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on $G$
528	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of $G$ as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
529	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety $X \times X$ (Hartshorne~II.6, or
530	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
531	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
532	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on $X \times X$. None of these has a characteristic
533	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
534	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
535	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
536	\end{remark}
537	
538	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
539	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
540	
541	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-$1$ ruling-out) goes
542	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
543	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
544	the extension fails at a prime divisor $W$ if and only if some affine coordinate of
545	$f$ around the image $\overline{f(\eta_W)}$ has a strictly negative order
546	$\ord_W(\cdot) < 0$. This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
547	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
548	
549	\begin{theorem}
550	
551	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
552	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
553	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:indeterminacy_locus,
554	        lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
555	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
556	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
557	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
558	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
559	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
560	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
561	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
562	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
563	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
564	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
565	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
566	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
567	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
568	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
569	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
570	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
571	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
572	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation $v_Y$ associated to a
573	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}$ as the
574	  % subring of $K(X)$ on which $v_Y \geq 0$) (read from
575	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
576	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its generic
577	  % point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at $\eta$ is a discrete
578	  % valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the function field of $X$. We call the
579	  % corresponding discrete valuation $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
580	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function on $X$.
581	  % Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has a \emph{zero} along
582	  % $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$ has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of
583	  % order $-v_Y(f)$."
584	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ and the order map).}
585	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$, let $Y$ be a variety over $\bar k$,
586	  let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map, and let $W \subseteq X$ be a
587	  prime divisor with generic point $\eta_W \in W$ (so
588	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1$). Let $(U, \varphi_U)$ be any representative of
589	  $f$ with $U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset$. The following are equivalent:
590	  \begin{enumerate}
591	    \item $f$ is defined at $\eta_W$ (i.e.\ $\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)$, equivalently
592	      $W \not\subseteq Z(f)$).
593	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood $V \subseteq Y$ of
594	      the generic image of $f$ near $W$, every regular function $g \in
595	      \mathcal O_Y(V)$ pulls back to a rational function $\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
596	      satisfying
597	      \[
598	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
599	      \]

600	  \end{enumerate}
601	
602	  Consequently: $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
603	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
604	  $W \subseteq X$ and every affine open $V \subseteq Y$ as above, the pullback
605	  $\varphi_U^\ast(g)$ has $\ord_W \geq 0$ for all $g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)$.
606	\end{theorem}
607	
608	\begin{proof}
609	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
610	        thm:codim_one_extension}
611	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}$ is a
612	  DVR with fraction field $K(X)$ and uniformiser determined by $W$; by
613	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly $\ord_W$. The local ring at
614	  $\eta_W$ is therefore characterised inside $K(X)$ as the subring
615	  \[
616	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
617	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
618	  \]
619	  The rational map $f$ is defined at $\eta_W$ if and only if, for any affine open
620	  $V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y$ around the image $\overline{f(\eta_W)}$,
621	  the pullback $\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)$ lands in
622	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}$: this is the standard local characterisation of where a
623	  rational map is regular at a codim-$1$ point of a smooth variety (it is the
624	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
625	  equivalent to $\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0$ for every $g \in A$, which is
626	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~$\Leftrightarrow$~(2) is therefore established.
627	
628	  For the consequence, $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor $W$ is
629	  contained in $Z(f)$, which by the equivalence above is iff the $\ord_W \geq 0$
630	  condition holds for every prime divisor $W$.
631	\end{proof}
632	
633	\begin{remark}
634	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
635	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
636	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
637	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
638	  isolation, the abstract codim-$\geq 2$ statement
639	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
640	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
641	  $\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}$ API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
642	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-$0$ classifying-map
643	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
644	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
645	\end{remark}

647	\section{Lean encoding}
648	\label{sec:codim1_lean_encoding}
649	
650	The Lean target file is the new
651	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean}. The declarations to
652	introduce, in order:
653	
654	\begin{enumerate}
655	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} ---
656	    \cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}, a method on the Mathlib
657	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure. Implementation: \texttt{X.carrier
658	    \textbackslash{} f.domain} as a \texttt{Set X}, paired with an
659	    \texttt{IsClosed} witness (either bundled or as a separate
660	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus} lemma).
661	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} ---
662	    \cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}, a \texttt{Prop}-valued predicate. Encoding:
663	    \texttt{$\forall$ $\eta$ : X, Order.coheight $\eta$ = 1 $\to$ $\eta$ $\in$
664	    f.domain} (matching the \texttt{Order.coheight} idiom of
665	    \cref{def:prime_divisor}).
666	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} ---
667	    \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, a lemma (or instance) producing
668	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing (Scheme.LocalRing.at\ X $\eta$)} under the
669	    typeclass set \texttt{[Smooth ($\Spec \bar k$) X] [IsIntegral X]} and the
670	    hypothesis \texttt{Order.coheight $\eta$ = 1}.
671	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_of\_codimOneFree\_of\_smooth}
672	    --- \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, the existence-of-extension theorem. Signature
673	    (informal): given a smooth variety $X$, a complete variety $Y$, and
674	    $f$~codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free, produce a unique regular morphism
675	    $\widetilde f \colon X \to Y$ extending $f$. Consumes
676	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} from
677	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as a Mathlib-style import.
678	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy\_pure\_codim\_one\_into\_grpScheme}
679	    --- \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}. Signature (informal): given a smooth
680	    variety $X$, a group scheme $G$ over $\bar k$ that is a variety (smooth, finite
681	    type, separated), and $f \colon X \dashrightarrow G$ rational, conclude that
682	    $Z(f)$ is either empty or of pure codimension $1$ in $X$.
683	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_iff\_order\_nonneg}
684	    --- \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}, the Weil-divisor reformulation. Re-uses
685	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order} from
686	    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} (the order map at a prime divisor).
687	\end{enumerate}
688	
689	\paragraph{Mathlib readiness audit.} At commit \texttt{b80f227} the prover should
690	verify:
691	\begin{itemize}
692	  \item \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain} is shipped at
693	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; its complement
694	    \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} is a one-line definition.
695	  \item The valuative criterion of properness in Mathlib form is
696	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.valuative\_criterion} (or analogous); the
697	    prover invokes this in Step~1 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof.
698	  \item Mathlib's smoothness predicate
699	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth} bundles the regular-local-rings hypothesis; the
700	    DVR-at-codim-1 lemma reduces to ``regular local of dim 1 = DVR''
701	    (\texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} or similar).
702	  \item Mathlib's product-of-smooth-is-smooth instance,
703	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.comp\_pullback} / equivalent, is needed in
704	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}'s Sub-step~4 to apply the Weil-divisor
705	    machinery to $X \times X$.
706	  \item The local-cohomology vanishing in Step~2 of
707	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof
708	    ($H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0$ at a
709	    depth-$\geq 2$ point) is a substantial Mathlib gap as of \texttt{b80f227}; the
710	    prover's options are (i) prove the depth-$\geq 2$ extension property as a local
711	    lemma using the \texttt{Module.depth} / Auslander--Buchsbaum machinery of
712	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}, or (ii) reformulate
713	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} in the weaker ``surface'' form
714	    (where $X$ has $\dim 2$, so codim-$\geq 2$ means closed points only and the
715	    extension is the classical Hartshorne~III.7.1 ``smooth-surface point removal''
716	    argument). For the consumer \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} only
717	    the surface case is needed in practice (the symmetric product $C^{(g)}$ is
718	    smooth, and the Abel--Jacobi map's intermediate-step indeterminacy lives on a
719	    smooth surface inside $C^{(g)} \times C^{(g)}$).
720	\end{itemize}
721	
722	No new core axiom is required. The proof of \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is
723	purely Weil-divisor manipulation on a smooth variety and is the cleanest part of
724	the chapter; the substance of the Lean engineering is in
725	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof, both halves of which rest on Mathlib API
726	gaps the prover must either fill in or work around.
727	
728	\section{Out of scope}
729	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
730	
731	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
732	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
733	scope:
734	\begin{itemize}
735	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular $\Rightarrow$
736	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
737	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
738	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
739	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
740	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
741	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
742	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
743	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
744	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
745	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
746	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
747	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
748	    $\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A$ / unirational$\dashrightarrow A$ constancy
749	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
750	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-$0$ base case is
751	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut
752	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
753	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
754	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on $X \times X$ for $X$ a curve.} The proof of
755	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
756	    smooth surface $X \times X$ but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
757	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-$1$
758	    decomposition $\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty$ on a nonsingular surface is
759	    used. The bilinear degree formula on $X \times X$ for $X$ a curve (intersection
760	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
761	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
762	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
763	    introduced.
764	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
765	    project's positive-genus arm runs over $\bar k$ throughout; descent of the
766	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
767	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
768	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
769	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
770	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
771	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for $G$ a group variety is sharper than any
772	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original $X$
773	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
774	\end{itemize}
775

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a $g$-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)$
20	on the category of $k$-schemes. Representing $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve $C \times_k T \to T$, which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras
23	$\mathcal{A}$, the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	$\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$. This chapter packages the construction
25	of $\underline{\Spec}_X(-)$ and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of $J$ (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here $S$ is a scheme, and $\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra. This means that $\mathcal{A}$ is a
49	  %  sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let $X$ be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf $\mathcal{A}$ of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras (i.e. an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
54	  equipped with $\mathcal{O}_X$-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is quasi-coherent. We write $\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)$ for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras and
58	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open $U = \Spec R \subseteq X$ one
83	sets $\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ regarded as an $R$-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because $\mathcal{A}$ is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for an inclusion
86	$V = \Spec S \subseteq U$ gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).

194	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
195	  Let $X$ be a scheme and $\mathcal{A}$ a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra.
196	  Then $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ represents the functor
197	  $F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$,
198	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
199	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$
200	  Equivalently, for any $X$-scheme $g : T \to X$,

205	  \]

207	  $\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T$ and $g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T$).
208	  The bijection is natural in $T$.
209	\end{theorem}

222	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /

229	  % Yoneda-bijection form pinned above (iter-174+ commitment; still pending
230	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
231	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
232	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor $F$ is a Zariski sheaf
233	  because morphisms of schemes and $\mathcal{O}$-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
234	  Choose an affine open cover $X = \bigcup U_i$. The subfunctor
235	  $F_i \subseteq F$ of pairs $(f, \varphi)$ with $f(T) \subseteq U_i$ is representable by
236	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))$ via the affine-base case
237	  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
238	  base-change identification $F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}$
239	  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}). Each $F_i \hookrightarrow F$ is an open
240	  subfunctor (pull back $U_i$ along $f$). The $F_i$ jointly cover $F$ since the $U_i$
241	  cover $X$. The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
242	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$
243	  of Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.

269	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra. Set $A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})$, regarded as an
270	  $R$-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of $X$-schemes
271	  \[
272	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
273	  \]
274	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
275	  spectrum of the global sections.
276	\end{theorem}

309	  Write $X = \Spec R$ and $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})$. Quasi-coherence of

317	  $a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'$. We verify $(\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})$
318	  represents $F$ on the affine base: given a test scheme $T$ and a pair
319	  $(f, \varphi)$, the composite
320	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
321	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
322	  is a ring map $A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$ and hence corresponds to a morphism
323	  $T \to \Spec A$ by the affine universal property
324	  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
325	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
326	  $(f, \varphi) \mapsto a$ defined by $f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a$,
327	  $\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}$.
328	\end{proof}

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi : X \to S$ a
36	projective morphism, $L$ a relatively very ample line bundle on $X$ over $S$,
37	and $E$ a coherent sheaf on $X$. The Quot construction packages the
38	$T$-flat coherent quotients $E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}$ of the
39	pull-back $E_T$ to $X_T = X \times_S T$, modulo the equivalence
40	$\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle$ defined by
41	$\ker(q) = \ker(q')$. Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	$\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$
43	(constant on connected $T$ by flatness; see~\S\ref{sec:hilbert_polynomial})
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$, each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective $S$-scheme.

3	\chapter{The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(P)$ and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of $\mathscr L(D)$); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,

15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z$, and the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~$0$
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); the global-sections
35	calculation $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a smooth
36	proper genus-$0$ curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(P)$ associated to a closed point $P \in C$
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its $\bar k$-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$
50	in the genus-$0$ specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that $C$ admits a non-constant rational function $f$
52	having at most a simple pole at $P$ and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	$C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ whose degree-$1$ identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$
64	% to vanish $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ from the degree count
65	% $\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0$. Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-$0$ hypothesis
75	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0$ and the skyscraper
76	% identity $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$. The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-$g$
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
82	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point is the one of
94	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order $\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	\Cref{def:principal_divisor} and \Cref{def:order_at_point}.

19	\[

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
32	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
33	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
34	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
35	derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
36	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
37	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
38	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that $\mathbb P^1$ is
39	\emph{already complete} and $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
40	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
41	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut} (the project's
42	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
43	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
44	\mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (the M\"obius scaling fixing both $0$ and
45	$\infty$), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb

# Blueprint Review Report

## Slug
iter184

## Iteration
184

## Top-level summaries

### Lean difficulty quality

- `Picard_RelativeSpec.tex` / `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}`:
  chapter prose pins a Yoneda-bijection
  $\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal A)) \cong \Hom_{\mathcal O_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal A, g_* \mathcal O_T)$,
  but the Lean target encodes the strictly weaker structural consequence
  `IsAffineHom (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)` (verified at
  `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean:264`). Documented intentionally
  by an in-chapter `% NOTE (iter-173 review)` block at L162 + an
  iter-179 supersede note at L220. Treat as **soon** — Lane D (iter-184)
  is closing `pullback_iso_construction` body, NOT the drift signatures.
  Carried over verbatim from iter-183 review (re-verified).

- `Picard_RelativeSpec.tex` / `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}`:
  Lean name `affine_base_iff` is misleading — the encoded type is the
  weaker `IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)` (verified at L311 of
  `RelativeSpec.lean`), NOT an iff. Documented in-chapter at L253.
  **Soon**; carry over from iter-183.

- `Picard_RelativeSpec.tex` / `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.base_change}`:
  Lean type is the weaker existential
  `∃ 𝒜', Nonempty (pullback ≅ T.RelativeSpec 𝒜')` (verified at L639
  of `RelativeSpec.lean`) rather than the canonical iso with a named
  pullback `g^* 𝒜`. Documented in-chapter at L338. **Soon**; carry over
  from iter-183.

All three findings remain `% NOTE (iter-173 review)`-flagged inside the
chapter; they are documented signature-drift items, NOT blueprint
correctness errors. Iter-184 Lane D is intentionally NOT touching them.

## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals

### Proposed chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`

**Covers**: `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean` (file does not
yet exist; per `STRATEGY.md` L143 the skeleton is owed iter-185+; this chapter
is the authoritative spec).
**Strategy phase**: A.3 — `Pic⁰` identity + degree (status "chapter pending;
substrate unowned" per `STRATEGY.md` L40; ~16–28 iters remaining)
**Why now**: Plan-phase status — a blueprint-writer dispatch
`pic0-identity-component-chapter` is fired in parallel with this audit
(directive at `.archon/logs/iter-184/blueprint-writer-pic0-identity-component-directive.md`),
but the chapter file has NOT landed by the time of this audit
(`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_IdentityComponent.tex` absent; writer
report `task_results/blueprint-writer-pic0-identity-component.md`
absent). Per directive item (4), the gate response is **deferred —
dispatched but not landed in time for this audit**. This is NOT a new
must-fix-this-iter; the iter-183 unstarted-phase proposal is being
addressed this plan-phase via the parallel writer dispatch. If the
writer lands the chapter during this iter's plan-phase before prover
objectives are written, the plan agent should re-dispatch
blueprint-reviewer scoped to `Picard_IdentityComponent.tex` only (the
same-iter fast path) before sending any A.3 prover. Otherwise the
chapter will be audited in iter-185's mandatory dispatch.

**Key declarations** (recap, in dependency order, from iter-183 proposal):
1. `\definition` `\label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}` —
   identity component of a group scheme `G/S` as open-and-closed subgroup
   scheme. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}`
   [expected]. Source: Stacks tag 0B7R / Milne III.6.
2. `\theorem` `\label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}` — for `G`
   locally of finite type over a field, `G⁰` is an open subgroup
   scheme. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}` [expected].
   Source: Milne III.6 / Stacks 0B7T.
3. `\definition` `\label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}` —
   `Pic⁰_{C/k} := (Pic_{C/k})⁰`. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}` [expected].
   Source: Milne III.6 Prop 6.1 / Kleiman §6.
4. `\definition` `\label{def:divisor_degree_pic}` — degree map
   `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ` via Hilbert-polynomial stratification.
   `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}` [expected].
   Source: Kleiman §5.
5. `\theorem` `\label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}` — `Pic⁰_{C/k}`
   is a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme of
   dimension `g(C)`. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}` [expected].
   Source: Milne III.6 Thm 6.4 / Hartshorne IV.4.

**`\uses` skeleton**:
- `thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` uses `def:identity_component_group_scheme`
- `def:pic_zero_subscheme` uses `def:identity_component_group_scheme`, `def:pic_scheme`
- `def:divisor_degree_pic` uses `def:pic_scheme`, `def:hilbert_polynomial`
- `thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety` uses `def:pic_zero_subscheme`,
  `thm:identity_component_open_subgroup`, `def:divisor_degree_pic`,
  `def:genus`, `thm:fga_pic_representability`

**Main theorem proof strategy**: identity-component extracts the
open-and-closed subscheme containing `η_J` from the disjoint-union
representability of `Pic_{C/k}` (Theorem 4.1 of
`Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex`). Dimension `g` by Riemann–Roch /
Serre duality on `Pic⁰` (Milne III.6); smoothness in char $p$ requires
care (Milne IV §13–14), in char 0 automatic. Geometric irreducibility
by definition. Degree map is the index of the Hilbert-polynomial
component (Kleiman §5).

**References for writer**:
- `references/abelian-varieties.pdf` (Milne *Abelian Varieties* 2008), §III.6,
  Prop 6.1 + Thm 6.4 — primary source for items 3 and 5.
- `references/kleiman-picard.pdf` (Kleiman *The Picard scheme*, FGA Explained
  arXiv:math/0504020), §5–§6 — primary source for items 1 and 4.
- `references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf` (Mumford *Abelian Varieties*),
  §III.6 + §III.13 — smoothness in char-$p$.
- Stacks tags 0B7R / 0B7T for the abstract identity-component
  construction — retrieval needed if `references/stacks-*.md` cache does
  not yet cover them; writer's `--write-domain` allows the
  reference-retriever dispatch.
- Optional: `references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf`, IV.4 (char-0
  Jacobian).

**Subphase choices exposed**:
- **Choice A vs Choice B**: Choice A (already committed in writer
  directive) builds `Pic⁰` as the abstract identity-component subgroup
  scheme via Stacks 0B7R/0B7T; Choice B would build it directly as a
  moduli functor of degree-zero line bundles. Trade-off: A inherits
  a substrate gap (`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` ~200–300 LOC, NEW
  PROJECT MATERIAL); B couples `Pic⁰`-definition to the degree map.
  **Already decided in directive: Choice A.**
- **Substrate split**: substrate stays in this chapter (with
  `% NOTE: future split candidate` marker), not split off into a
  separate `GroupScheme_IdentityComponent.tex` sibling.
  **Already decided in directive: keep substrate in chapter.**
